title: Reducing household packaging waste
subtitle: Practical approaches to packaging-free and lower-packaging shopping
excerpt: UK households generate approximately 100 kg of packaging waste per person per year. Reduction strategies range from refill shops and bulk buying to reading recycling labels accurately and avoiding materials that cannot actually be processed at the kerbside.
sourceNotes: WRAP UK Household Packaging Report (2022). RECOUP UK Household Plastics Collection Survey. Wrap Recycle Now guidance on what can and cannot be kerbside-recycled.

glossaryTerms.definition: Collection of materials at the doorstep for processing. In the UK, kerbside recycling typically accepts paper and card, glass bottles and jars, aluminium and steel cans, and some plastics (bottles and trays in most areas). Flexible plastics, black plastic trays, and composite materials are generally not accepted.

A model keeping materials in use. Packaging reduction sits above recycling in the waste hierarchy: reduction prevents the material existing in the first place; recycling recovers it after use. Both have a role.

The carbon in the material production of a package. Aluminium cans have high embodied carbon to produce but very high recycling rates. Glass is lower-carbon to produce but heavier, with higher transport emissions. Paper and card have lower embodied carbon than most plastics but more water in production.

content.text: The packaging waste hierarchy: avoid first, then reduce, then choose recyclable materials, then recycle. Most packaging reduction conversations begin at recycling, skipping the higher rungs. Approximately 44% of UK kerbside-collected plastic is actually recycled into new products; the remainder is either exported or disposed of. Understanding what is verifiably recyclable locally changes purchasing decisions.
Check what your council actually collects

Recycling labels on packaging say 'widely recycled', 'check locally', or 'not yet recycled'. 'Widely recycled' means accepted by approximately 75% or more of UK councils. 'Check locally' means accepted by fewer. The only reliable way to know is to look up your specific council's accepted materials list, which differs meaningfully between authorities. 'Wishcycling' (putting non-accepted materials in the recycling bin hoping they will be processed) contaminates loads and can cause the entire batch to be rejected.
rate (70%+), can be recycled indefinitely. Heavy, so higher transport carbon than plastic for the same volume of product. Worth choosing for foods stored for months.
Aluminium: 90%+ recycling rate for cans in the UK. Recycling aluminium uses 5% of the energy of primary production. Buy canned goods in aluminium rather than lined cartons (Tetrapak) where possible.
Card and paper: widely recyclable. Avoid wax-coated, foil-lined, or plastic-laminated card (shiny takeaway boxes, some cereal bag liners), which cannot be recycled with standard card.

Flexible plastic film: carrier bags, bread bags, crisp packets, and plastic film wrapping are not collected at the kerbside by most UK councils. Many supermarkets provide in-store collection for clean, dry flexible plastic bags only.

Refill shops allow washing-up liquid, shampoo, cleaning products, dried goods, and some wet goods to be brought in existing containers and refilled. The available network is patchy: urban areas have more options. For dried goods (oats, rice, pulses, flour, nuts), buying in larger bags from a wholesaler or cash-and-carry reduces packaging per kg of product and is typically cheaper. 5 kg bags produce approximately 60-70% less packaging by weight than the equivalent quantity in 500 g bags.

attrs.title: Compostable packaging
attrs.body: Packaging labelled 'compostable' or 'biodegradable' requires industrial composting conditions (55-70°C for several weeks) to break down. Most UK councils do not accept it in food waste or garden waste collections, and it should not go in a home compost bin or wormery. Without industrial processing, 'compostable' packaging behaves like standard plastic in the environment. Check your council's food waste processing method before assuming this packaging is environmentally neutral.

content.text: principle applied to packaging: choose glass or metal where recycling actually works, avoid flexible plastic where alternatives exist, and reduce overall quantity by buying in bulk. The
of packaging varies widely by material, but the most impactful change is reducing how much enters the home in the first place.
